name: Vic-Fezensac and Bazian Loop
name_original: Vic-Fezensac et Bazian – boucle dans le Pays d'Auch
description: This difficult 31.9-mile touring cycling loop through the Auch Country offers green hills, vineyards, and picturesque villages.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Vic-Fezensac traverse a landscape characterized by rolling green hills, extensive vineyards, and the valleys of Armagnac. The region features varied terrain, from gentle paths along waterways like the Baïse River to more challenging routes with notable elevation changes. Cyclists will encounter medieval bastides and historic villages nestled within the countryside. Best touring cycling routes around Vic-Fezensac The most popular touring cycling route is Vic-Fezensac and Bazian Loop, a 31.9 miles (51.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 12 minutes to complete. This route winds through green hills and vineyards, offering a scenic experience. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Fourcès, the round village – Fourcès loop from Bezolles, a difficult 41.3 miles (66.4 km) path. This route explores the countryside around the historic village of Fourcès. Local touring cyclists also love the Lectoure – Château de Terraube loop from La Croutz, a 92.4 miles (148.8 km) trail leading through the wider Gers region, often completed in about 9 hours 23 minutes. Touring cycling around Vic-Fezensac is defined by rolling green hills, vineyards, and historic bastides. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easier paths to more demanding routes. The routes in Vic-Fezensac are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 60 reviews. More than 900 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Vic-Fezensac's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Vic-Fezensac?
A: There are over 45 touring cycling routes around Vic-Fezensac listed on komoot, offering a wide variety of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Vic-Fezensac offers a few easy touring cycling routes. For instance, the Armagnac Greenway, though not listed in the provided routes, is known as an old railway line redeveloped into a well-maintained path ideal for family rides, offering views of vineyards and fortified villages. While most listed routes are more challenging, the region generally provides options for various ability levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while cycling in Vic-Fezensac?
A: You'll cycle through picturesque landscapes characterized by rolling green hills, extensive vineyards, and charming countryside. The region is part of the Gers, known for its hilly terrain. You can also find routes along waterways like the Baïse River, showcasing historic mills, and through the valleys of Armagnac with pleasant scenery between countryside and forest.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes in Vic-Fezensac are designed as loops. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021115750/vic-fezensac-and-bazian-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vic-Fezensac and Bazian Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which winds through green hills and vineyards. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/29358851" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lac de Lupiac loop from Castillon-Debats&lt;/a&gt;, offering a scenic ride around the lake.

Q: What historical sites or attractions can I visit along the cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in history and culture. You can cycle past medieval bastides and authentic villages such as Larressingle, Fourcès, and Montréal-du-Gers. Notable attractions include the beautifully preserved &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7076323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Flaran Abbey&lt;/a&gt;, a 12th-century Cistercian monastery. The town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6047982" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vic-Fezensac&lt;/a&gt; itself has historical points of interest like the Chapter Tower and half-timbered houses.

Q: Are there more challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists?
A: Absolutely. The Vic-Fezensac area offers 27 difficult touring cycling routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40933215" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lectoure – Château de Terraube loop from La Croutz&lt;/a&gt;, a substantial 148.8 km (92.4 miles) trail with significant elevation changes, leading through the wider Gers region.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Vic-Fezensac?
A: The region's pleasant climate generally makes spring and early autumn ideal for touring cycling, when temperatures are moderate and the landscapes are vibrant with greenery or autumn colors. Summers can be warm, but the many shaded paths, such as the Sentier de Baisse, can offer relief.

Q: Can I find places to eat or rest along the routes?
A: Yes, many cycling routes pass through charming villages and towns where you can find local cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel and rest. Exploring authentic villages like Larressingle or Fourcès often provides opportunities for a break and a taste of local gastronomy.

Q: Is it possible to rent bikes in the Vic-Fezensac area?
A: Yes, bike hire services are available in the region, including electric bikes. This is particularly helpful for tackling the area's rolling hills and varied terrain, making touring cycling accessible to a wider range of abilities.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about cycling in Vic-Fezensac?
A: The touring cycling routes in Vic-Fezensac are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 60 reviews. Cyclists often praise the scenic beauty of the green hills and vineyards, as well as the charm of the historic bastides and villages encountered along the way.

Q: Are there any routes that follow waterways or rivers?
A: Yes, the Baïse Valley Cycle Route (Vélo-route des moulins) is a notable option that follows the Baïse river. This route showcases historic mills and impressive locks, such as the double lock of Graziac, and connects to typical bastides like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3957544" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valence-sur-Baïse&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable villages or bastides to explore by bike?
A: The region is dotted with authentic villages and medieval bastides perfect for exploring by bike. These include Larressingle, often integrated into routes offering panoramic views, and Fourcès, known for its unique circular layout. &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3553918" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lupiac&lt;/a&gt;, the birthplace of D'Artagnan, is another charming village you might encounter.
tips: Castelneau-type Gers village with a castle built on a rocky outcrop, old half-timbered houses and narrow grassy streets. Many hiking departures.

Small town in the Pays d'Auch, known for its Feria de la Pentecôte, the Pentecotavic, during which a superb festival of brass bands takes place. To see: the Chapter Tower, the Cordeliers convent, the arenas and the few half-timbered houses that remain in the town centre.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: This touring bicycle loop is approximately 51.4 km long and generally takes around 3 hours and 12 minutes to complete. However, this duration can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as 'difficult' and requires very good fitness due to its 480 meters of elevation gain over 51.4 km. While the terrain is mostly paved and suitable for all skill levels in terms of bike handling, the significant climbs make it challenging for beginners. It's best suited for experienced touring cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Vic-Fezensac and Bazian loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly on paved surfaces, making it suitable for touring bicycles. You can expect a 'slightly hilly' landscape with numerous climbs throughout the ride, characteristic of the Gers region. The roads are generally quiet with almost no traffic.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience picturesque 'green hills' and lush 'Armagnac vineyards.' The route offers beautiful views of authentic villages and medieval bastides where time seems to slow down. It showcases the 'rural landscapes of French Tuscany,' featuring orchards, vineyards, grain fields, and forests, with the distant Pyrenees often visible.

faqs.question: Are there any notable towns or highlights along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through the charming town of Bazian and the significant town of Vic-Fezensac. Vic-Fezensac is known for its bullfighting traditions and Landes races, and it's also part of the wider Baïse Valley Cycle Route.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Vic-Fezensac and Bazian loop?
faqs.answer: The Gers region is beautiful throughout the warmer months. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer. These seasons also provide vibrant landscapes with blooming flowers or rich autumn colors in the vineyards.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Vic-Fezensac and Bazian loop?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, Vic-Fezensac is a major town along the route and a likely starting point. You should be able to find public parking within the town to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is the Vic-Fezensac and Bazian loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a touring bicycle route primarily on public roads. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, it's important to consider their safety and comfort if you plan to bring them along on a bike trailer. Always ensure they are secured and have access to water.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, this cycling route uses public roads and paths in the Auch Country. You do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this loop.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several local paths. You will find yourself on sections of the Chemin de Picaouzet, Carrelot de la Gleisa, Carrelot de la Riu, Chemin de Cassagneux, Rue du Lavoir, and Carrelot deu Putz for short distances.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments or accommodation nearby?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Vic-Fezensac and Bazian are villages where you can expect to find local amenities such as cafes, restaurants, and potentially accommodation options. Vic-Fezensac, being a larger town, will offer more choices for refreshments and places to stay.
